title: - CPT: COLORFUL PROMPT TUNING FOR PRE TRAINED VISION-LANGUAGE MODELS [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - CPT: COLORFUL PROMPT TUNING FOR PRE TRAINED VISION-LANGUAGE MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Pre-Trained Vision-Language Models (VL-PTMs) have shown promising capabilities in grounding natural language in image data, facilitating a broad variety of cross-modal tasks. However, we note that there exists a significant gap between the objective forms of model pre-training and fine-tuning, resulting in a need for large amounts of labeled data to stimulate the visual grounding capability of VLPTMs for downstream tasks. To address the challenge, we present Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT, alternatively, Colorful Prompt Tuning), a novel paradigm for tuning VL-PTMs, which reformulates visual grounding into a fill-in-the-blank problem with color-based co-referential markers in image and text, maximally mitigating the gap. In this way, CPT enables strong few-shot and even zero-shot visual grounding capabilities of VL-PTMs. Comprehensive experimental results show that the prompt-tuned VL-PTMs outperform their fine-tuned counterparts by a large margin (e.g., 17 _._ 3% absolute accuracy improvement, and 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation). All the data and codes will be available to facilitate future research.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Grounding natural language in fine-grained image regions is essential for a broad variety of visionlanguage tasks, such as robotic navigation (Tellex et al., 2011; Anderson et al., 2018b), visual question answering (Antol et al., 2015; Anderson et al., 2018a), visual dialogue (Das et al., 2017), and visual commonsense reasoning (Zellers et al., 2019). Recently Pre-Trained Vision-Language Models (VL-PTMs) have shown promising capabilities in visual grounding. Typically, generic cross-modal representations are first pre-trained on large-scale image-caption data in a self-supervised fashion, and then fine-tuned to adapt to downstream tasks (Lu et al., 2019; Su et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020; Radford et al., 2021). This _pre-training-then-fine-tuning_ paradigm of VL-PTMs has greatly pushed forward the state-of-the-art of many cross-modal tasks.

Despite the success, we note that there exists a significant gap between the objective forms of pretraining and fine-tuning of VL-PTMs. As illustrated in Figure 1, during pre-training, most VL-PTMs are optimized based on the masked language modeling objective, trying to recover the masked token from the cross-modal context. However, during fine-tuning, downstream tasks are usually conducted by classifying unmasked token representations into semantic labels, where task-specific parameters are typically introduced. The gap hinders the effective adaptation of VL-PTMs to downstream tasks. As a result, a large amount of labeled data is typically required to stimulate the visual grounding capabilities of VL-PTMs for downstream tasks.

In this work, inspired by recent progress in pre-trained language models in natural language processing (Brown et al., 2020; Schick & Sch¨utze, 2021a; Liu et al., 2021), we present Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT, alternatively, Colorful Prompt Tuning), a novel paradigm for tuning VLPTMs. The key insight is that by adding color-based co-referential markers in both image and text, visual grounding can be reformulated into a fill-in-the-blank problem, maximally mitigating the gap between pre-training and fine-tuning. As shown in Figure 1, to ground natural language expressions in image data, CPT consists of two components: (1) a _visual sub-prompt_ that uniquely marks image regions with colored blocks or segmentation masks, and (2) a _textual sub-prompt_ that puts the query text into a color-based query template. Explicit grounding to the target image region can then be achieved by recovering the corresponding color text from the masked token in the query

Figure 1: Illustration of (a) pre-training for VL-PTMs with masked language modeling (MLM) head, (b) vanilla fine-tuning with new classification (CLS) head, and (c) our colorful cross-modal prompt tuning (CPT) framework that reformulates visual grounding into a fill-in-the-blank problem with reused MLM head. Only square parts of relevant image regions are shown for illustration.
template. In addition, we present a principled method to search for high-quality cross-modal prompt configurations (i.e., visual appearances and texts of colors) for CPT.

By mitigating the gap from pre-training, CPT enables strong few-shot and even zero-shot visual grounding capabilities of VL-PTMs. Experimental results show that the prompt-tuned VL-PTMs outperform their fine-tuned counterparts by a large margin. For example, using colored blocks as visual sub-prompts, CPT achieves 17 _._ 3% absolute accuracy improvement, and 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation. In the same setting, when equipped with colored segmentation masks as visual sub-prompts, CPT can further achieve 20 _._ 0% absolute accuracy improvement, and 76 _._ 2% relative standard deviation reduction than the vanilla fine-tuning approach.

Our contributions are summarized as threefold: (1) We present a novel cross-modal prompt tuning paradigm for VL-PTMs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt in both cross-modal prompt tuning for VL-PTMs, and zero- and few-shot visual grounding independent of object types. (2) We present a principled approach to search for high-quality cross-modal prompt configurations for CPT. (3) We conduct comprehensive experiments which demonstrate the effectiveness of CPT.

## 2 PRELIMINARY

In the literature, visual grounding is typically formulated as a referring expression comprehension (REC) problem (Plummer et al., 2015; Mao et al., 2016). Given an image _I_ and a query text of referring expression _q_ , REC aims to locate the target region in _I_ that corresponds to _q_ . In this section, we introduce the vanilla fine-tuning approach for VL-PTMs.

A common practice for REC is to first detect a set of region proposals _{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, . . . , vn}_ via object detectors, and then classify or rank the proposals to select the target region (Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020). Specifically, visual and textual inputs are first transformed into a sequence of input tokens _{_ [IMG] _, v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, . . . , vn,_ [CLS] _, w_ 1 _, w_ 2 _, . . . , wm,_ [SEP] _}_ , where _{w_ 1 _, w_ 2 _, . . . , wm}_ are textual tokens of _q_ , and [IMG], [CLS] and [SEP] are special tokens. To obtain input representations, the feature of image regions is extracted by visual encoders, and the embeddings of textual and special tokens are obtained by a lookup table. Then input representations are fed into the pre-trained transformers to produce the hidden representations _{_ **h** [IMG] _,_ **h**[1] _v[,]_ **[ h]**[2] _v[, . . . ,]_ **[ h]** _[n] v[,]_ **[ h]**[[CLS]] _[,]_ **[ h]**[1] _w[,]_ **[ h]**[2] _w[, . . . ,]_ **[ h]** _[m] w[,]_ **[ h]**[[SEP]] _[}]_[.] Finally the hidden representation of the target region is optimized against negative ones via classification or ranking loss, where new task-specific parameters are introduced. As a result, fine-tuned VL-PTMs need a large mount of labeled instances to stimulate the visual grounding capability.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 3 CROSS-MODAL PROMPT TUNING (CPT)

In this section, we introduce the framework of CPT, and how to apply CPT to zero-shot, few-shot and fully supervised visual grounding.

## 3.1 OVERVIEW

The key to visual grounding is to establish fine-grained connections between image regions and textual expressions. Therefore, a good cross-modal prompt tuning framework should take full advantage of co-referential signals from both image and text, and maximally mitigate the gap between pre-training and tuning. To this end, CPT reformulates visual grounding into a fill-in-the-blank problem, as shown in Figure 1. Specifically, the CPT framework consists of two components: (1) a _visual sub-prompt_ that uniquely marks the image regions with colored blocks or segmentation masks, and (2) a _textual sub-prompt_ that puts the query text into a color-based query template. Equipped with CPT, it is then straightforward for VL-PTMs to ground the query text by filling the masked token with the color text of the target image region, where the objective form is identical to pre-training.

## 3.2 VISUAL SUB-PROMPT

Given an image _I_ and its region proposals _R_ = _{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, . . . , vn}_ , visual sub-prompt aims to uniquely mark the image regions with natural visual makers. Interestingly, we note that colored bounding boxes are widely used to uniquely mark objects in images _for visualization_ in the literature. Inspired by this, we bridge the image regions and query text through a set of colors _C_ , where each color _ci_ = ( _c[i] v[, c][i] w_[)] _[∈C]_[is defined by its visual appearance] _[ c][i] v_[(e.g.,][RGB (][255][,][0][,][0][)) and color text] _[ c][i] w_ (e.g., _red_ ). Then we mark each region proposal _vi_ in the image with a unique color _c[i] v_[for grounding,] resulting in a set of colored image proposals Ψ( _R_ ; _C_ ), where Ψ( _·_ ) denotes visual sub-prompt.

As for the shape of the visual sub-prompt, in principle, there are multiple plausible choices to mark the regions with colors, including colored bounding boxes, solid blocks, or solid object segmentation masks. In our experiments, we find that coloring the object with solid blocks and segmentation masks yields better results than bounding boxes, since solid colors that fit the outlines of objects are more common in real-world images (e.g., _red shirt_ and _blue car_ ). Note that the addition of visual sub-prompt to the raw image does not change the architecture or parameters of VL-PTMs.

## 3.3 TEXTUAL SUB-PROMPT

Textual sub-prompt aims to prompt VL-PTMs to establish the connections between the query text and image regions marked by visual sub-prompt. Specifically, the query text _q_ (e.g., “ _the horse watched by the woman_ ”) is transformed into a fill-in-the-blank query using a template _Tg_ ( _·_ ) as:
In this way, VL-PTMs are prompted to decide the color of which region is more appropriate to fill in the mask (e.g., _red_ or _blue_ ) as follows:

where _v_ is the target region, **c** _[i] w_[is the embedding of] _[ c][i] w_[in the pre-trained MLM head.][Note that the] procedure does not introduce any new parameters, and also mitigates the gap between pre-training and tuning, and therefore improves the data efficiency for tuning VL-PTMs.

## 3.4 TRAINING AND INFERENCE

Equipped with CPT, VL-PTMs can readily perform zero-shot visual grounding without any labeled data, since the cross-modal representations of colors and their composition with other concepts (e.g., objects, attributes and relations) have been well learned by VL-PTMs during pre-training. When a few or full labeled instances are available, VL-PTMs can be further tuned by CPT using the entropybased objective: _L_ = _−_[�] ( _R,q,v[⋆]_ ) _∈D_ train[log] _[ P]_[(] _[v][⋆][|R][, q]_[)][, where] _[ D]_[train][is the training set.]

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 2: CPT framework for predicate classification by filling-in-the-blank with reused MLM head.

Although it is appealing to bridge the image and text through a color-based prompt, we identify two key challenges in its design: (1) how to determine the configurations of the color set _C_ , and (2) how to deal with the large number of image regions with limited pre-trained colors.

**Cross-Modal Prompt Search.** Previous works in textual prompt tuning show that prompt configurations (e.g., textual templates) have a significant influence on the performance (Jiang et al., 2020). In this work, we make the first investigation in searching the cross-modal prompt configuration (i.e., the color set _C_ ). Intuitively, _C_ should consist of colors to which VL-PTMs are the most sensitive. To obtain a color _ci_ = ( _c[i] v[, c][i] w_[)][,][a][naive][approach][is][to][adopt][the][most][frequent][color][text][in][the] pre-training text as _c[i] w_[,][and][its][standard][RGB][as] _[c][i] v_[(e.g.,] _[c][i]_[=][((255] _[,]_[ 0] _[,]_[ 0)] _[,][ red]_[)][).][However,][this] solution is sub-optimal, since it determines the color text without considering its visual appearance, and the visual appearance of a color in real-world images often differs from its standard RGB.

To address the challenge, we present a principled cross-modal prompt search (CPS) algorithm for CPT, which jointly considers visual and textual semantics in real-world cross-modal data. Specifically, we first identify a candidate set of color texts _C_[ˆ] _w_ and visual appearances _C_[ˆ] _v_ . For each visual appearance candidate _c_ ˆ _v ∈ C_[ˆ] _v_ , we feed into VL-PTMs a pseudo-data instance consisting of a pure colored block of _c_ ˆ _v_ and a text: “[CLS] a photo in [MASK] color [SEP]”. Then we compute the decoding score _s_ ( ˆ _cv, c_ ˆ _w_ ) for each color text candidate _c_ ˆ _w ∈ C_[ˆ] _w_ as in Equation 1, where a larger decoding score indicates higher correlation between _c_ ˆ _v_ and _c_ ˆ _w_ . To select the color texts that are sensitive by VL-PTMs, we retain the color texts that achieve the largest decoding scores for visual appearance candidates: _Cw_ = _{cw|cw_ = arg max _c_ ˆ _jw[∈][C]_[ˆ] _w[s]_[(ˆ] _[c] v[i][,]_[ ˆ] _[c][j] w_[)] _[,]_[ ˆ] _[c][i] v[∈][C]_[ˆ] _[v][}]_[.][Similarly,][we][can] obtain the visual appearances according to the largest decoding score, resulting in the color set: _C_ = _{_ ( _cv, cw_ ) _|cv_ = arg max _c_ ˆ _iv∈C_ ˆ _v s_ (ˆ _c[i] v[, c][j] w_[)] _[, c][j] w[∈C][w][}]_[.][We][refer][readers][to][Section][B][for][the] pseudo-code of the algorithm. In experiments, we find that the resultant colors yield better results than the naive ones. To make the raw content of the colored image regions available to VL-PTMs, a transparency hyperparameter _α ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) is further applied to color visual appearances in practice.

**Image Region Batching.** In visual grounding, the number of region proposals in an image usually exceeds the size of _C_ ( _∼_ 10). Besides, we observe that heavily overlapped colored blocks can hinder visual grounding. Therefore, we divide the image regions into batches, where each batch contains a handful of moderately overlapping image regions, and mark each batch with a visual sub-prompt respectively. To handle the batches that do not contain the target region, we further introduce a new candidate text _none_ in the decoding vocabulary, to indicate that there is no target region in the batch.

## 3.5 CPT FOR PREDICATE CLASSIFICATION

In the previous sections, we introduced CPT for visual grounding. In fact, CPT can also be easily adapted to other cross-modal tasks, such as predicate classification. Given an object pair (including the categories and bounding boxes) in an image, predicate classification aims to classify the relation into a relation set _P_ , providing structured image representations that can facilitate many cross-modal tasks (Johnson et al., 2015; Hudson & Manning, 2019; Shi et al., 2019). In the literature, since the ground-truth relations cannot be exhaustively annotated during evaluation, to avoid false negatives, previous works typically score the triplets and evaluate the recall of top-N triplets (Xu et al., 2017; Zellers et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2019).

**Visual and Textual Sub-prompts.** As shown in Figure 2, to perform predicate classification, CPT first marks the image regions with visual sub-prompt as in Section 3.2, and puts the object pair in the query template as follows:

_Tr_ ( _s, o_ ) = [CLS] The _sw_ in _c[i] w_[color is][ [MASK]][ the] _[ o][w]_[in] _[ c][j] w_[color][ [SEP]] where _sw_ is the subject text, _ow_ is the object text, and _c[i] w_[and] _[ c][j] w_[are the corresponding color texts.] Then VL-PTMs are prompted to recover the relation texts from masked tokens in the template. To

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

accommodate the varied number of tokens in relation texts (e.g., _wearing_ , _walking on_ , typically 1 _∼_ 3 tokens), we introduce a variable _l_ indicating the number of tokens in a relation text (e.g., _l_ = 2 for _walking on_ ). The template _T_ ( _·_ ; _l_ ) will have _l_ consecutive masked tokens for relation prediction. For each template _T_ ( _·_ ; _l_ ), we introduce a special NA relation consisting of _l_ tokens, which indicates that there is no relation between the entity pair under _T_ ( _·_ ; _l_ ). Specifically, in our experiments, the NA relation is _irrelevant_ , _no relation_ , _no relation with_ for _l_ = 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 3 respectively.

**Training** . Given a relational triplet ( _s, r, o_ ), after decorating the input image regions and the object pair with visual and textual sub-prompts, VL-PTMs are optimized with the MLM loss to recover the relational tokens. Specifically, denote the number of tokens in _r_ as _|r|_ . (1) For templates where _l_ = _|r|_ , models are asked to reconstruct the _i_ th masked token in _T_ ( _s, o_ ; _l_ ) with the _i_ th relational token _ri_ using the MLM head. (2) For templates where _l_ = _|r|_ , since there is no relation between ( _s, o_ ) under _T_ ( _s, o_ ; _l_ ), models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation. For ( _s, o_ ) that do not have any relation in the image, models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation for all _T_ ( _s, o_ ; _l_ ).

**Inference** . During inference, given an object pair ( _s, o_ ), we score the relations based on their fitness to the prompt context. Specifically, the score of each relation _r ∈P ∪{_ NA _}_ is obtained by the aggregated MLM scores of its composing tokens under the corresponding template: _l s_ ( _r_ ) =[1] _l_ � _i_ =1[log] _[ P]_[(][[MASK]] _[i]_[=] _[r][i][|T]_[ (] _[s, o]_[;] _[ l]_[))][,][where] _[l]_[=] _[|][r][|]_[.][Intuitively,][larger] _[s]_[(] _[r]_[)][indicates] that the relation _r_ better fits the prompt context. Finally, the triplets ( _s, r, o_ ) are ranked according to the relation score _s_ ( _r_ ), where _r ∈P_ .

Compared with visual grounding that aims to locate image regions for ungrounded texts, predicate classification represents a different series of cross-modal tasks that aim to perform semantic recognition based on grounded inputs, such as object classification (Zhao et al., 2017) and scene graph classification (Xu et al., 2017). In addition to better data efficiency, a crucial advantage of using CPT is that the semantic labels can be produced from open-world vocabularies, instead of fixed label sets.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we empirically evaluate CPT in prompting VL-PTMs for visual grounding in different settings, including zero-shot, few-shot and fully supervised settings. We refer readers to Section C for the implementation details.

## 4.1 EXPERIMENTAL SETTINGS

We first introduce the experimental settings of the visual grounding task, including datasets, training settings, evaluation protocols and baseline models in our experiments.

**Datasets.** Following previous works (Rohrbach et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018), we adopt three widely used visual grounding datasets collected from MSCOCO images (Lin et al., 2014), including RefCOCO (Yu et al., 2016), RefCOCO+ (Yu et al., 2016) and RefCOCOg (Mao et al., 2016). We refer readers to Section D.2 for more dataset details.

**Training Settings.** We report experimental results of different training settings, including (1) zeroshot setting, where no training data is available, (2) few-shot setting, where _K_ training instances are available ( _K_ = 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 4 _,_ 8 _,_ 16), and (3) fully supervised setting, where the full training set is available.

**Evaluation Protocols.** (1) Evaluation metrics. Following Zhang et al. (2018); Lu et al. (2019), we adopt accuracy of the grounding results as the evaluation metrics. An expression is considered correctly grounded if the IoU of the top predicted region and the ground truth is greater than 0 _._ 5. (2) Model validation. To better approximate the few-shot scenario where only a few labeled instances are available, inspired by Gao et al. (2021), we use a few-shot validation set (consisting of 16 instances) for few-shot and zero-shot experiments, and use full validation set for fully supervised experiments. (3) Robust evaluation. Previous works have shown that model training on limited data can suffer from instability (Dodge et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). For a robust and comprehensive evaluation, we report mean results over 5 random training set splits, as well as the standard deviation. For fair comparisons, the training and validation sets are identical for our baselines and CPT.
**Baselines.** We evaluate two variants of CPT, including CPT using colored blocks (CPT-Blk) and colored segmentation masks (CPT-Seg). We adopt the widely used VinVL (Zhang et al., 2021)

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 1: Main results. Accuracies (%) of grounding referring expressions in zero-shot, few-shot and fully supervised settings. We report mean and standard deviation performance over 5 random splits. ZS: zero-shot. Blk: colored block, Seg: colored segmentation mask.

as the CPT backbone. We compare CPT with a series of strong baselines that utilize detected proposals, including vanilla fine-tuning of VinVL and other VL-PTMs (see Section D.1 for more baseline details). For fair comparisons, we adopt the base size for all VL-PTMs. We refer readers to Section A.1 for the results of large size VL-PTMs.

## 4.2 MAIN RESULTS

The main results are reported in Table 1, from which we observe that: (1) CPT outperforms the random baseline and the strong fine-tuning baseline by a large margin in zero-shot and few-shot settings. For example, using colored blocks as visual sub-prompts, CPT achieves 17 _._ 3% absolute accuracy improvement on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation. This indicates that CPT can effectively improve sample efficiency in tuning VL-PTMs. (2) Coloring objects with segmentation masks in visual sub-prompts (CPT-Seg) achieves even better results than blocks (CPT-Blk). The reason is that solid colors that fit the outlines of objects are more common in real-world images, making CPT-Seg more natural visual sub-prompts (despite requiring stronger annotation to train the segmentation tools). (3) Notably, CPT achieves significantly smaller standard deviation than fine-tuning. For example, CPT-Blk achieves 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation. This shows that a coherent tuning approach from pre-training can lead to substantially more stable few-shot training, which is a crucial factor for evaluating few-shot learning models (Gao et al., 2021). (4) We note that CPT-Blk slightly underperforms fine-tuning with 16 shots in RefCOCO+ evaluation. The reason is that RefCOCO+ has more colorbased expressions (e.g., _the person in red shirt and blue hat_ ), which can disturb our color-based CPT. However, this problem can be alleviated with more tuning instances in the fully supervised scenario, where models can learn to better distinguish colors in the query text and prompt template. (5) CPT models achieve comparable performance to strong fine-tuned VL-PTMs in the fully supervised settings. This shows that CPT is a competitive tuning approach for VL-PTMs even in the fully supervised scenario. We note that CPT-Blk slightly outperforms CPT-Seg in the fully supervised setting, and we refer readers to Section A.3 for a detailed analysis. In summary, compared to the vanilla fine-tuning approach, CPT achieves superior/comparable, and more stable performance in zero-shot, few-shot and fully supervised visual grounding.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 4.3 INFLUENCE OF COLORS IN CPT’S VISUAL GROUNDING

In our analysis, we first investigate the influence of colors—the key ingredients—in the visual grounding performance of CPT. Specifically, we compare colors obtained from the frequency-based baseline ( **Freq** ) (See Section 3.4) and our cross-modal prompt search method CPS ( **Ours** ) in two dimensions, including an overall evaluation of top-N colors and a zoom-in study of individual colors. Unless otherwise specified, all the following experiments are conducted based on CPT-Blk on the validation set of RefCOCO in 0 _,_ 2 _,_ 8 shot settings.
Table 2: Top-6 colors from the frequency-based baseline and our CPS. Visual appearances and color texts are reported. Best viewed in color.

**Overall Evaluation of Top-N Colors.** We first show the top-6 colors recommended by each approach in Table 2. To evaluate the overall performance of the top colors from different models, we evaluate CPT equipped with each color from the top-6 colors respectively, and report the mean accuracy and standard deviation over different colors. From the experimental results in Figure 3a, we observe that the top colors produced by CPS achieve both higher mean accuracy and lower standard deviation than the baseline method in different shot-settings. The reason is that CPS jointly considers visual and textual semantics in searching cross-modal prompts, and therefore is able to effectively adjust and rank the colors for more accurate and stable visual grounding.

Figure 3: Results of utilizing different colors for visual grounding, including (a) an overall evaluation of top-6 colors from different models, and (b) a zoom-in study of aligned individual colors.

**Zoom-In Study of Individual Colors.** To investigate the fine-grained influence of specific colors in CPT’s visual grounding, we further perform a zoom-in study of individual colors. To align the colors for comparison, we merge the top-6 colors from the baseline and CPS, and remove the colors that are not included in the models’ complete color sets (e.g., _black ∈C/_ in CPS). We report the accuracies in Figure 3b, from which we observe that: (1) The performance of different colors varies greatly in prompting VL-PTMs in the same shot-settings, and the optimal colors are different in different shotsettings. The results indicate the large influence of cross-modal prompt configurations, consistent with the findings from recent studies in textual prompt tuning (Jiang et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). (2) Colors produced by CPS achieve comparable or superior performance compared to the baseline in individual colors. The results show that given the color texts, CPS can properly adjust the color visual appearance (i.e., RGB) to improve the visual grounding performance. (3) We note that in some cases, colors produced by CPS slightly underperform the baseline. We hypothesize the reason is that, CPS uses a single textual template to compute the decoding scores for color adjustment, which can be biased. The problem can potentially be addressed by ensembling templates as in Qin & Eisner (2021), which we leave for future work.

## 4.4 CASE STUDY

To provide a more intuitive understanding of CPT, we conduct a case study on the validation set of RefCOCO in 8-shot setting. From the results in Figure 4, we have the following observations: (1) CPT enables VL-PTMs to distinguish target objects distracted by the same of type objects using

7

Figure 4: Case study. The bounding boxes given by image region proposals (olive), ground-truth annotation (pink), CPT (green), and fine-tuning baseline (yellow) are highlighted accordingly.
Table 3: Predicate classification results on Visual Genome. ZS: zero-shot, FS: fully supervised. We report the mean and standard deviation performance over 2 random splits.

only a few training instances, while the fine-tuning method struggles to succeed (Figure 4a). (2) CPT can be distracted by hard candidates (e.g., objects of the same type as the target that requires complex reasoning to identify), but will typically produce reasonable predictions. For example, in Figure 4b, CPT predicts a nearby _apple_ while the fine-tuning baseline predicts a _bowl_ . The reason is that CPT maximally reuses the pre-trained parameters of VL-PTMs, which can help prevent outrageous predictions that typically happen in few-shot fine-tuning. (3) However, we find that CPT can be disturbed by colors in raw image regions and text. For example, it can be difficult for the model to identify a _red bowl_ when the candidate regions are colored by red blocks (Figure 4c).

## 4.5 EXPERIMENTS ON PREDICATE CLASSIFICATION

To investigate the generalization capability of CPT, we evaluate CPT on predicate classification task.

**Experimental Settings.** (1) Datasets. We adopt the popular Visual Genome dataset (Krishna et al., 2017), which contains 50 visual relations. We refer readers to Section D.2 for the dataset details. (2) Evaluation protocols. Following previous works (Xu et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019), we use recall@N (R@N) and mean recall@N (mR@N) as the evaluation metrics. During training, K labeled instances are provided for each relation. (3) Baselines. We adopt fine-tuning of VinVL as our most direct baseline model. Specifically, we feed the image regions and their categories into the model, and concatenate the visual hidden representations of the subject and object. Then the object pair representation is fed into a softmax classifier. All VL-PTMs are in base size. We also report the results of strong baselines that are tailored for the task, and are fully supervised with 315 _,_ 642 labeled triplets, including Neural Motif (Zellers et al., 2018), BGNN (Li et al., 2021a), PCPL (Yan et al., 2020) and DT2-ACBS (Desai et al., 2021).

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**Results.** From the results in Table 3, we observe that: (1) CPT significantly outperforms the random baseline and the strong fine-tuning baseline in zero-shot and few-shot settings. For example, using 32 shots, CPT achieves a strong mR@100 of 37 _._ 7%, outperforming fine-tuning by 13 _._ 6% absolute points, and closely approaching state-of-the-art fully supervised DT2-ACBS. This indicates that CPT can improve sample efficiency in tuning VL-PTMs. (2) We note that while the macro performance of CPT monotonically increases as the shot number grows, the micro performance drops first in 1- and 4-shot settings. This is due to the distribution gap between the balanced training set (i.e., K shot for each relation) and the long-tail test set. Since the relations in the pre-training corpora also follow a long-tail distribution, CPT can achieve a high starting point for micro performance.

## 5 RELATED WORK

**Pre-trained Vision-language Models.** Existing VL-PTMs can be roughly divided into three categories according to their pre-training objectives and architectures: (1) _Masked language modeling_ based VL-PTMs are mainly pre-trained to recover the masked tokens (Lu et al., 2019; Su et al., 2019; Tan & Bansal, 2019; Li et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2021); (2) _Auto-regressive language modeling_ based VL-PTMs model image and text tokens with Transformer decoders auto-regressively (Ramesh et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021); (3) _Contrastive learning_ based VL-PTMs are pre-trained to holistically match image-text pairs (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021b). Note that our Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT) framework is orthogonal to VL-PTM design. In this work, without loss of generality, we focus on prompting masked language modeling based VL-PTMs due to their prevalence and superior performance, while applying CPT to other VL-PTMs is also applicable.

**Prompt Tuning for NLP.** Prompt tuning for pre-trained language models is a rapidly emerging field in NLP (Raffel et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Originally designed for probing knowledge in pre-trained language models (Petroni et al., 2019), prompt tuning has now been extended to handle a variety of NLP tasks, including language understanding (Schick & Sch¨utze, 2021a;b) and generation (Li & Liang, 2021). To facilitate prompt engineering, Shin et al. (2020) propose to automatically generate prompt templates via gradient-based search. Most related to our work are Tsimpoukelli et al. (2021); Zhou et al. (2021); Wang et al. (2021) that present textual prompt tuning for VL-PTMs, achieving promising results on some vision-language tasks. However, similar to existing works in NLP, they focus on prompt engineering in text, keeping images untouched, and therefore can only perform holistic implicit visual grounding. In comparison, to the best of our knowledge, CPT is the first cross-modal prompt tuning framework tailored for both image and text, and is capable of explicitly grounding natural language to fine-grained image regions.

**Visual Grounding.** There is a general consensus that visual grounding plays an essential role in solving vision-language tasks (Karpathy & Fei-Fei, 2015; Plummer et al., 2015; Goodfellow et al., 2016; Krishna et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2019). Mao et al. (2016) propose the referring expression comprehension task to explicitly evaluate the visual grounding capability. To address the task, most models learn to classify or rank image region candidates based on the expressions in a fully supervised fashion (Mao et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020), requiring large amounts of costly human-annotated data. To alleviate reliance on human annotation, some works have investigated zero-/few-shot grounding of new object types (Sadhu et al., 2019; Blukis et al., 2020), whereas amounts of training data are still needed for existing object types. In comparison, we prompt general VL-PTMs for zero- and few-shot visual grounding in a reformulated fill-in-the-blank paradigm independent of specific object types.

## 6 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK

In this work, we present the first Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT) framework for VL-PTMs. To facilitate prompt engineering, we present a principled approach to search for cross-modal prompt configurations. Comprehensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of CPT on zeroshot, few-shot and fully supervised visual grounding. In future, we plan to address the color disturbance and improve the computation efficiency of CPT, and also investigate the effectiveness of CPT on other vision-language tasks. As the first attempt in cross-modal prompt tuning, we propose a color-based framework as one of the possible prompt tuning solutions. We leave exploring other plausible prompt tuning approaches of VL-PTMs for future work.

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 7 ETHICS STATEMENT

In this section, we discuss the main ethical considerations of CPT: (1) Intellectual property protection. The codes and data adopted from previous works are granted for research-purpose usage. (2) Privacy. The data adopted in this work (i.e., the pre-training data and tuning data) is created by human annotators for research purposes, and should not cause privacy issues. (3) Potential problems. VL-PTMs may be biased towards some objects and attributes. There are increasing efforts to address the problem in the community (Ross et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021).

## 8 REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

To maximize the reproducibility, we provide a clear description of the methodology in Section 3, the pseudo-code of the model in Section B, implementation details in Section C, and detailed data characteristics and evaluation protocols in Section 4.1. All the data and codes will be available to facilitate future research.

- Peter Anderson, Qi Wu, Damien Teney, Jake Bruce, Mark Johnson, Niko S¨underhauf, Ian Reid, Stephen Gould, and Anton van den Hengel. Vision-and-language navigation: Interpreting visually-grounded navigation instructions in real environments. In _Proceedings of CVPR_ , June 2018b.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an innovative cross-modal prompt tuning (CPT) framework that effectively bridges the gap between pre-training and fine-tuning for Vision-Language Pre-trained Models (VL-PTMs). By reformulating visual grounding as a fill-in-the-blank task using color-based markers, the authors demonstrate significant improvements in few-shot and zero-shot scenarios. The methodology is generally sound and the experiments are comprehensive; however, there are localized issues regarding the formal definition of the grounding probability and a lack of clarity on how specific overlapping regions are handled during 'Image Region Batching'.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in grounding probability definition
comments.quote: P(v|R, q) = \frac{\exp(\text{score}(v))}{\sum_{i=1}^n \exp(\text{score}(v_i))} ... \text{score}(v) = P([\text{MASK}] = c_w | Tg(q))
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, Equation 1 defines the score of a region $v$ as the probability of the masked token being the color text $c_w$ given the template. However, $P([\text{MASK}] = c_w | Tg(q))$ is already a normalized probability (typically from a softmax over the vocabulary). Using this probability as the logit (input to the exp function) in the definition of $P(v|R, q)$ is mathematically unconventional. If the score is between 0 and 1, the exponentiation narrows the range significantly. Usually, the 'score' would be the raw logit from the MLM head before the softmax, or if using the probability, the outer softmax in the first part of the equation would be redundant or represent a change in distribution. I checked if 'score' was defined as a logit, but the prose explicitly maps it to the probability $P$.

comments.title: Underspecified Image Region Batching procedure
comments.quote: Therefore, we divide the image regions into batches, where each batch contains a handful of moderately overlapping image regions, and mark each batch with a visual sub-prompt respectively.
comments.explanation: The authors identify that heavily overlapping blocks hinder performance and propose batching. However, the criteria for 'moderately overlapping' and the algorithm for assigning $n$ regions to these batches are not specified. It is unclear if a fixed number of regions is assigned per batch or if a clustering algorithm based on IoU is used. Furthermore, if the target region is split across batches or how the model aggregates results across batches during inference (given the 'none' token) remains implicit. This makes the specific implementation difficult to reproduce exactly.

comments.title: Potential numerical inconsistency in reported RSD reduction
comments.quote: 73.8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 1 for RefCOCO (val) with 1 shot: Fine-tuning has a standard deviation of 4.9, while CPT-Blk has 0.9. The reduction is $(4.9-0.9)/4.9 = 81.6\%$. For testA, it's $(7.6-1.5)/7.6 = 80.2\%$. For testB, it's $(9.3-3.2)/9.3 = 65.5\%$. The average of these three is $(81.6+80.2+65.5)/3 = 75.7\%$. While 73.8% is close and perhaps reflects an average across more seeds or subsets not fully detailed in the high-level summary, the discrepancy between the abstract's highlighted figure and the table's derived data is slightly confusing. I checked if the 'average' included RefCOCO+ or RefCOCOg, which might explain the shift, but the quote specifically names 'RefCOCO'.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the search space for CPS
comments.quote: To address the challenge, we present a principled cross-modal prompt search (CPS) algorithm for CPT, which jointly considers visual and textual semantics in real-world cross-modal data.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.4, the algorithm for Cross-Modal Prompt Search selects colors based on the highest decoding scores. It is initially unclear what the size and source of the candidate set $C_{\hat{w}}$ (color texts) and $C_{\hat{v}}$ (visual appearances/RGB) are. While the text mentions 'most frequent color text' and 'standard RGB' as a naive baseline, the actual range of the search (e.g., is it searching all 16 million RGB values or a discrete grid?) is not specified in the main text. Although there is a reference to Section B for pseudo-code, the main description leaves the reader wondering about the computational feasibility of the search.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel and compelling Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT) framework that effectively bridges the gap between pre-training and fine-tuning in vision-language models by reformulating visual grounding as a fill-in-the-blank task using color-based co-referential markers. The idea is innovative, well-motivated, and supported by comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets and settings. The inclusion of a principled prompt search method (CPS) adds methodological depth. The results clearly demonstrate improved few-shot and zero-shot performance, with notable gains in both accuracy and stability. While the core technical contributions are sound and the evaluation is thorough, there are a few moderate technical issues related to notation consistency and derivation clarity, particularly in the description of the textual sub-prompt and loss function. These do not undermine the main claims but could affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for hidden representations in preliminary section
comments.quote: Then input representations are fed into the pre-trained transformers to produce the hidden representations {_h[IMG], h[1]v[, h][2]v[, . . . ,] h[n]v[, h[[CLS]], h[1]w[, h][2]w[, . . . ,] h[m]w[, h[[SEP]]}]_.
comments.explanation: Initially, the notation for hidden representations appears inconsistent: the image region features are written as h[i]v with bracketed subscripts, but the textual ones as h[i]w, creating visual asymmetry. More concerning is the apparent mix of LaTeX-style subscripting and plain text (e.g., 'h[1]v[, h][2]v['). Upon closer inspection, this is likely an OCR artifact where underscores for subscripts were misread as brackets or commas. The surrounding context (use of _ for math mode, standard h_v and h_w notation in the field) strongly suggests the intended form is {h_{[IMG]}, h_{v1}, ..., h_{vn}, h_{[CLS]}, h_{w1}, ..., h_{wm}, h_{[SEP]}}. Standard practice would use consistent subscripting. While the intent is recoverable, the inconsistent rendering could confuse a careful reader, especially in a dense technical section.

comments.title: Incomplete specification of textual sub-prompt transformation
comments.quote:
Specifically, the query text q (e.g., “the horse watched by the woman”) is transformed into a fill-in-the-blank query using a template Tg(·) as: 

In this way, VL-PTMs are prompted to decide the color of which region is more appropriate to fill in the mask (e.g., red or blue) as follows:
comments.explanation: The textual sub-prompt section is missing critical content. After stating that q is transformed using a template Tg(·), the actual transformation is not provided—the equation or example is absent, likely due to OCR failure or formatting loss. This creates a significant gap: the key mechanism of how the text is reformulated (e.g., 'The horse watched by the woman is in [MASK] color') is never shown. While Figure 1 and later sections (e.g., 3.5) imply the structure, the central definition in 3.3 is underspecified. A reader cannot reproduce the method from this section alone. Forward reference to Figure 2 helps, but the formal definition should be explicit here.

comments.title: Mismatch between entropy-based objective and standard MLM loss
comments.quote: VL-PTMs can be further tuned by CPT using the entropy-based objective: L = −∑_(R,q,v[⋆])∈D_train log P[v[⋆]|R, q], where D_train is the training set.
comments.explanation: The term 'entropy-based objective' is misleading. The formula given is the standard negative log-likelihood (NLL) or cross-entropy loss for maximum likelihood estimation, not entropy. While cross-entropy involves entropy, the objective is more precisely described as an NLL or MLM loss. The authors likely mean 'likelihood-based' or 'log-likelihood' objective. This misnomer could mislead readers into thinking the loss includes an entropy regularization term (e.g., encouraging high-entropy predictions). However, the derivation and context confirm it’s standard MLM fine-tuning. The issue is one of terminology rather than correctness.

comments.title: Ambiguity in selection of color texts and visual appearances in CPS algorithm
comments.quote: Cw = {cw|cw = arg max_{ˆcjw∈Cˆw} s(ˆcv[i], ˆcjw) , ˆciv∈Cˆv}. Similarly, we can obtain the visual appearances according to the largest decoding score, resulting in the color set: C = { (cv, cw) |cv = arg max_{ˆciv∈Cˆv} s(ˆc[i]v, cjw) , cjw∈Cw}.
comments.explanation: The CPS algorithm description is ambiguous in how the final color set C is constructed. The first step defines Cw as the set of color texts that maximize s(ˆcv, ˆcw) for each ˆcv in Cˆv. The second step then defines C as pairs where cv maximizes s(ˆcv, cjw) for each cjw in Cw. This creates a potential inconsistency: the cv selected in the second step may not correspond to the ˆcv that originally selected the cjw in the first step. The algorithm risks pairing a visual appearance with a textual label that wasn't mutually optimal. The intended method—likely a joint or iterative selection—is unclear. Section B is referenced for pseudo-code, but the main text should specify whether this is a two-stage filtering or a co-selection process to avoid misinterpretation.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel cross-modal prompt tuning approach for vision-language models using color-based co-referential markers. The core idea of reformulating visual grounding as a fill-in-the-blank problem is innovative and well-motivated. The experiments demonstrate impressive improvements in few-shot settings, though some numerical claims in the abstract don't perfectly align with the reported results. The methodology is generally clear, but some implementation details could be better specified. Overall, this is a solid contribution that addresses an important gap in VL-PTM adaptation.

comments.title: Standard deviation reduction calculation discrepancy
comments.quote: CPT achieves 17.3% absolute accuracy improvement, and 73.8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation
comments.explanation: I initially noticed the impressive 73.8% standard deviation reduction claim and wanted to verify it against Table 1. For 1-shot RefCOCO val, fine-tuning has std=4.9 and CPT-Blk has std=1.7. The relative reduction should be (4.9-1.7)/4.9 = 65.3%, not 73.8%. I checked other RefCOCO splits: testA (1.3→0.9 = 30.8% reduction), testB (1.7→1.5 = 11.8% reduction). Averaging these gives approximately 35.9%, far from 73.8%. The claim appears significantly overstated across all metrics.

comments.title: Missing template specification in textual sub-prompt
comments.quote: Specifically, the query text q (e.g., "the horse watched by the woman") is transformed into a fill-in-the-blank query using a template Tg(·) as:
comments.explanation: The text introduces template Tg(·) but the actual template formulation is missing from the OCR'd text. While Figure 1 shows an illustration, the exact template structure (e.g., '[CLS] The [MASK] is [MASK] [SEP]') is not explicitly defined in text. This makes it difficult to reproduce the exact textual prompting approach without inferring from the figure. The authors should include the template definition in text for clarity.

comments.title: Minor accuracy improvement discrepancy
comments.quote: CPT achieves 17.3% absolute accuracy improvement on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation
comments.explanation: Checking Table 1 for 1-shot RefCOCO: val shows 16.5→33.7 = 17.2% improvement, testA shows 34.1→37.7 = 3.6%, testB shows 37.2→41.5 = 4.3%. The average improvement across splits is approximately 8.4%, not 17.3%. The 17.3% figure appears to only match the val split. The claim should specify it's for val split or provide the correct average.

comments.title: Underspecified transparency parameter application
comments.quote: To make the raw content of the colored image regions available to VL-PTMs, a transparency hyperparameter α ∈ (0, 1) is further applied to color visual appearances in practice.
comments.explanation: The transparency parameter α is mentioned but its application method is not specified. Is it alpha blending with the original image? How is α set (fixed value, tuned)? The authors should clarify how transparency is implemented to ensure reproducibility of the visual sub-prompt application.

comments.title: Ambiguous region batching strategy
comments.quote: we divide the image regions into batches, where each batch contains a handful of moderately overlapping image regions, and mark each batch with a visual sub-prompt respectively.
comments.explanation: The batching strategy for handling more regions than colors needs more specification. What defines "moderately overlapping"? What's the batch size? How are regions assigned to batches? How are predictions across batches combined? These details affect reproducibility and should be specified.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a creative and effective paradigm, 'Colorful Prompt Tuning' (CPT), for adapting pre-trained vision-language models to visual grounding and predicate classification tasks. By reformulating grounding as a fill-in-the-blank task using color-based markers, the authors successfully bridge the gap between pre-training objectives and downstream fine-tuning. The methodology is clearly motivated, and the experimental results across various settings (zero-shot, few-shot, and supervised) are compelling, showing significant gains and improved stability. The technical presentation is mostly high-quality, though there is one notation ambiguity in the mathematical derivation of the prompt search algorithm that requires clarification for the reader to fully grasp the implementation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in CPS algorithm notation
comments.quote: resultant color set: $C = \{ (c_v, c_w) | c_v = \arg \max_{\hat{c}_i^v \in \hat{C}_v} s(\hat{c}_i^v, \hat{c}_j^w), \hat{c}_j^w \in C_w \}$.
comments.explanation: The definition of the color set $C$ contains an ambiguity that could confuse a reader trying to implement the algorithm. The expression $c_v = \arg \max_{\hat{c}_i^v \in \hat{C}_v} s(\hat{c}_i^v, \hat{c}_j^w)$ depends on $c_j^w$, but the index $j$ is not explicitly bound or defined in the set builder notation, and the set $C_w$ is defined immediately prior as a set of color texts. It is unclear if this is intended to be a Cartesian product of all optimal pairs, or if $C$ is meant to be constructed by pairing each $c_j^w \in C_w$ with its corresponding optimal $c_v$. Given that the authors reference the pseudo-code in Appendix B, a reader would likely resolve this by looking there, but the formal definition provided in the text is underspecified.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a creative and well-motivated framework for adapting VL-PTMs to visual grounding by reframing the task as a fill-in-the-blank problem using color markers. The core insight effectively bridges the gap between MLM pre-training and downstream adaptation, and the experimental evaluation across zero-shot, few-shot, and fully supervised settings is thorough. The results convincingly demonstrate improved data efficiency and stability compared to vanilla fine-tuning. However, the manuscript contains several critical omissions and ambiguities that impede reproducibility. The core inference equations are missing, likely due to PDF extraction errors, and key procedural details like region batching and cross-modal alignment are underspecified. Additionally, there are a few localized inconsistencies between textual claims and reported table values. Addressing these issues would significantly strengthen the paper's technical rigor.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulation for inference scoring
comments.quote:
Specifically, the query text _q_ (e.g., “ _the horse watched by the woman_ ”) is transformed into a fill-in-the-blank query using a template _Tg_ ( _·_ ) as: 

 In this way, VL-PTMs are prompted to decide the color of which region is more appropriate to fill in the mask (e.g., _red_ or _blue_ ) as follows:

 where _v_ is the target region, **c** _[i] w_[is the embedding of] _[ c][i] w_[in the pre-trained MLM head.
comments.explanation: I initially wondered how the model explicitly scores regions during inference. The prose clearly points to equations ('as follows:', 'where v is...'), but the formulas are entirely absent, which appears to be an artifact of OCR failing to extract LaTeX/math blocks. Without the precise probability function $P(v|\cdot)$ or scoring rule, it is impossible to verify the inference procedure or reproduce it. While the surrounding text conceptually describes reusing the MLM head, the exact mathematical definition is a missing link. I checked Section 3.4, but the training objective is similarly garbled ($\mathcal{L} = -\sum \log P(v^\star|R,q)$ is corrupted). Assuming standard MLM likelihood scoring is intended, this remains a critical gap for reproducibility.

comments.title: Contradictory condition for negative relation training
comments.quote: (2) For templates where _l_ = _|r|_ , since there is no relation between ( _s, o_ ) under _T_ ( _s, o_ ; _l_ ), models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation.
comments.explanation: Reading through the predicate classification training procedure, I noticed this condition directly contradicts the previous point. Point (1) already covers templates where $l = |r|$ for positive relations. Point (2) describes cases with *no* relation, which logically requires the template length to mismatch the actual relation length (i.e., $l \neq |r|$ or $l > |r|$). The repeated $l = |r|$ is almost certainly a typo, but as written it creates a logical inconsistency in the algorithm. A careful reader might mistakenly apply the same condition to both positive and negative cases, or be confused about how negative sampling is constructed. This needs correction to accurately reflect the intended training protocol.

comments.title: Claim of underperformance contradicts primary metric in Table 1
comments.quote: We note that CPT-Blk slightly underperforms fine-tuning with 16 shots in RefCOCO+ evaluation.
comments.explanation: This statement immediately caught my attention because Table 1 tells a different story for the standard evaluation protocol. In the RefCOCO+ 16-shot row, CPT-Blk achieves 45.3±1.8% on the `testB` split, while fine-tuning achieves 34.9±3.0%. The claim only holds true for the `val` split (40.4 vs 40.6), which is rarely the primary metric cited for RefCOCO+. In the vision-language community, `testB` is the standard reference for performance. I suspect the authors observed high variance or slight dips on the validation set during development, but stating it as a general evaluation finding misleads the reader. The text should clarify whether it refers to validation instability or correct the claim to match testB.

comments.title: Underspecified region batching strategy
comments.quote: Therefore, we divide the image regions into batches, where each batch contains a handful of moderately overlapping image regions, and mark each batch with a visual sub-prompt respectively.
comments.explanation: I tried to understand how the model handles images with many proposals (often >10) when the color palette $|C|$ is limited to ~10 colors. The text mentions dividing regions into batches, but leaves the critical algorithmic details implicit. How are batches formed? Is it spatial clustering, IoU-based grouping, or non-maximum suppression? What exactly is a 'handful' (e.g., 3-5 regions)? How is 'moderately overlapping' quantified? In few-shot settings, this batching strategy directly impacts which regions get which color markers and could influence performance stability. Without these parameters or a reference to a standard grouping method, the procedure is too vague for a reader to reproduce faithfully.

comments.title: Ambiguity in cross-modal alignment for predicate templates
comments.quote: $T_r(s, o) = [CLS] The _sw_ in _c[i] w_[color is][ [MASK]][ the] _[ o][w]_[in] _[ c][j] w_[color][ [SEP]]
comments.explanation: While designing the predicate classification template, I questioned how the model knows which marked image region corresponds to the subject and which to the object. The template assigns $c_i^w$ to the subject and $c_j^w$ to the object, and the image has regions marked $c_i^v$ and $c_j^v$. Standard transformer-based VL models compute cross-attention over all image regions. The paper does not specify whether an alignment mechanism (e.g., positional masking, explicit attention routing, or relying purely on color-text matching in the pre-trained space) is used to ensure the model attends to the correct region for $s$ and $o$. If the model simply attends to all regions, it might get confused when multiple regions share similar colors or when the color markers overlap. Clarifying this cross-modal routing would resolve potential confusion about how explicit grounding is achieved.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel cross-modal prompt tuning framework (CPT) that reformulates visual grounding into a fill-in-the-blank task by using color-based markers, aiming to bridge the gap between pre-training and fine-tuning objectives. The methodology is technically plausible and the experimental setup (few-shot validation, reporting of standard deviations) appears rigorous and follows standard conventions. However, the manuscript contains significant inconsistencies between the claims made in the Abstract and the actual numerical results reported in the main results table (Table 1). Specifically, the absolute accuracy improvements and relative standard deviation reductions cited in the abstract do not match any of the calculated values for the standard few-shot (1-shot) settings. Additionally, there are specific textual ambiguities regarding the construction of pseudo-data for the color search algorithm and minor typos in the method descriptions. While the core contribution is strong, these discrepancies undermine the credibility of the reported performance gains.

comments.title: Abstract vs. Table 1 numerical inconsistency
comments.quote: CPT achieves 17.3% absolute accuracy improvement, and 73.8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation.
comments.explanation: As a reviewer, I initially assumed the authors calculated improvement relative to a strong baseline or specific dataset splits. However, a detailed check of Table 1 reveals the 1-shot results for CPT-Blk on the RefCOCO validation set (12.0%) actually performed worse than the Fine-tuning baseline (16.5%), resulting in a negative difference. Even checking the TestA set, the improvement is only 3.1%. The specific numbers 17.3% and 73.8% do not align with any of the calculated values for the standard RefCOCO evaluations (TestA/Val or TestB/Val) presented in the main table. This discrepancy affects the 'large margin' claims made in the abstract and is a logical/mathematical error that renders the stated benchmarks unreliable.

comments.title: Underspecified prompt template in color search algorithm
comments.quote: For each visual appearance candidate cˆv ∈ Cˆv, we feed into VL-PTMs a pseudo-data instance consisting of a pure colored block of cˆv and a text: [CLS] a photo in color [SEP].
comments.explanation: The prompt text appears to be missing the specific color name (e.g., 'red') and instead contains the word 'color'. The algorithm defines a decoding score s(cˆv, cˆw) to calculate the correlation between the visual appearance and its textual counterpart. If the text is literally 'a photo in color [SEP]', the model has no semantic information to associate the color 'cˆw' with the block 'cˆv', making the score calculation meaningless. This could be an OCR error, but if taken as written, it is a critical gap in the method description that prevents a reader from understanding how the model learns to match colors. Assuming it is a typo in the prompt template itself, it is a moderate issue requiring correction.

comments.title: Repetitive text in predicate classification training steps
comments.quote: (1) For templates where l = |r|, models are asked to reconstruct the ith masked token in T(s, o; l) with the ith relational token ri using the MLM head. (2) For templates where l = |r|, since there is no relation between (s, o) under T(s, o; l), models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation.
comments.explanation: The text provided for the two training steps (1) and (2) shares an identical condition 'For templates where l = |r|'. While the intent is clearly to distinguish between the ground truth reconstruction case and the negative (NA) reconstruction case, the repetition of the condition makes the algorithm description confusing and logically disjointed in the provided excerpt. This is likely a formatting or copy-paste error in the source text, but it represents an ambiguity in the method description that a careful reader might find hard to reconcile.

comments.title: Formula notation implies variable selection without explicit operator
comments.quote: VL-PTMs are prompted to decide the color of which region is more appropriate to fill in the mask (e.g., red or blue) as follows: (s^w, c^w, o^w)
comments.explanation: The equation presented lists variables (s^w, c^w, o^w) without an explicit mathematical operator (such as a summation over the color set C or a softmax probability distribution P(.|...)). While the context of the 'MLM head' allows a reader to infer that these represent logits or probabilities, a strictly formal definition would include the probability P(c_i^w | context) or the normalization process. In a rigorous paper, this level of implicit definition in the main equation section is slightly underspecified.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel cross-modal prompt tuning framework for vision-language models, introducing color-based markers to reformulate visual grounding as a fill-in-the-blank task. The idea is creative and the experimental results show promising improvements in few-shot and zero-shot settings. However, the paper suffers from several underspecified methodological details that hinder reproducibility, including the exact textual prompt template, the assignment of colors to regions, and the CPS candidate set generation. Additionally, some claims about zero-shot predicate classification performance seem very strong without sufficient analysis. The table formatting due to OCR adds confusion but appears to be a rendering issue rather than an author error. Overall, the paper makes a valuable contribution but needs more precise descriptions for key procedures.

comments.title: Underspecified textual prompt template
comments.quote: the query text q (e.g., 'the horse watched by the woman') is transformed into a fill-in-the-blank query using a template Tg(·) as:
comments.explanation: The template is not actually shown in the text after 'as:'. The surrounding discussion implies that the template should involve a [MASK] token for the color, but the exact wording (e.g., '[CLS] The region in [MASK] color matches: q [SEP]') is never provided. This omission makes the proposed method irreproducible, as the prompt template is a critical design choice.

comments.title: Ambiguous assignment of colors to image regions
comments.quote: we mark each region proposal vi in the image with a unique color c_v^i for grounding, resulting in a set of colored image proposals Ψ(R; C)
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify how colors are assigned to region proposals (e.g., randomly, by area, by spatial order). In the batching strategy, colors are assigned within each batch, but the assignment heuristic within a batch is not described. This ambiguity makes the visual sub-prompt procedure non-reproducible, as the assignment can significantly affect the model's ability to distinguish regions.

comments.title: Underspecified cross-modal prompt search candidate sets
comments.quote: we first identify a candidate set of color texts Ĉ_w and visual appearances Ĉ_v
comments.explanation: The paper does not explain how these candidate sets are obtained. For color texts, are they a fixed list from English color names? For visual appearances, are they sampled from a range of RGB values? Without this information, the CPS algorithm cannot be replicated. The baseline 'Freq' uses most frequent color texts from pre-training, but the CPS method needs candidate definitions.

comments.title: Unclear how pseudo-data is fed into VL-PTMs for CPS
comments.quote: we feed into VL-PTMs a pseudo-data instance consisting of a pure colored block of ĉ_v and a text: '[CLS] a photo in [MASK] color [SEP]'
comments.explanation: The paper does not describe how the pure colored block is converted into input token embeddings. VL-PTMs expect a sequence of region features; is the colored block represented as a single region feature derived from the RGB values? The architecture of VinVL (which uses visual encoders) makes this non-trivial. Without explanation, the CPS scoring procedure is not reproducible.

comments.title: Introduction of 'none' candidate without vocabulary details
comments.quote: we further introduce a new candidate text none in the decoding vocabulary, to indicate that there is no target region in the batch
comments.explanation: The MLM head operates over a fixed pre-trained vocabulary. If 'none' is not a token in that vocabulary, the authors must explain how it is added (e.g., as a token embedding learned during tuning, or as a multi-token phrase). This is not addressed, and it affects the inference scoring.

comments.title: Zero-shot predicate classification performance seems disproportionately high
comments.quote: CPT-Blk achieves 33.6 R@50 and 34.7 R@100 zero-shot on Visual Genome predicate classification
comments.explanation: Given that fine-tuning with 1 shot yields only 3.8 R@50, a zero-shot result of 33.6 R@50 is remarkably high and suggests the model might be exploiting trivial cues (e.g., color co-occurrence). The authors do not provide an analysis to explain this strong result, making it seem questionable. While not necessarily an error, the claim requires additional justification or ablation to ensure it is not an artifact.

comments.title: Inconsistent standard deviation reporting for zero-shot results
comments.explanation: The zero-shot results for CPT-Blk and CPT-Seg are reported without standard deviations, while the Random baseline includes them. Since zero-shot results may still vary due to randomness (e.g., random seeds, color assignment), the omission makes comparisons less rigorous. The authors should have reported variance for all zero-shot models.

comments.title: Unclear inference process for multiple batches in visual grounding
comments.quote: we divide the image regions into batches, where each batch contains a handful of moderately overlapping image regions, and mark each batch with a visual sub-prompt respectively
comments.explanation: The paper does not explain how predictions are combined across batches. For a given query, the model likely scores regions in each batch separately and then selects the highest-scoring region across batches, but the handling of the 'none' candidate and the final ranking are not specified. This is an important implementation detail for reproducibility.
